preamble.summary: Psalm 47 is a brief, exuberant enthronement psalm of the Sons of Korah celebrating God's kingship over all the earth. The psalm opens with a call for all peoples to clap their hands and shout, declares God as the Most High and great King, and culminates in God ascending his throne amid shouts and the blast of the shofar. The nations gather as the people of the God of Abraham, and God is exalted as ruler over all.
preamble.remarkable: Psalm 47 is the primary psalm associated with Rosh Hashanah in Jewish liturgy — it is recited seven times before the shofar blasts. The psalm's universalism is striking: it does not merely claim that God rules Israel but that all peoples (kol ha-ammim) are summoned to praise him, and that the nobles of the peoples gather as the people of the God of Abraham (v. 10). The promise to Abraham that 'in you all the families of the earth will be blessed' (Genesis 12:3) finds liturgical expression here. The phrase alah Elohim bitru'ah ('God has gone up with a shout,' v. 6) envisions a divine enthronement procession — God ascending to his throne like a king entering his palace. In Christian liturgy, this verse was applied to the Ascension of Christ.
preamble.friction: The term Elyon ('Most High,' v. 3) is an ancient Canaanite divine title adopted into Israelite theology (cf. Genesis 14:18-20, Deuteronomy 32:8). Its use here reinforces God's supremacy over all peoples and all other claimed deities. The phrase am Elohe Avraham ('the people of the God of Abraham,' v. 10) rather than 'the people of God' or 'Israel' is deliberate — Abraham is the patriarch of many nations (Genesis 17:4-5), not just Israel. The inclusion of 'nobles of the peoples' (nedive ammim) in God's people pushes past ethnic boundaries. The verb alah ('to go up,' v. 6) may reflect a liturgical ritual in which the Ark of the Covenant was carried up to the Temple mount, symbolizing God's enthronement.
preamble.connections: The enthronement psalms (47, 93, 95-99) share the declaration 'The LORD reigns' (or its equivalent). Psalm 47 connects to Psalm 24:7-10 (the gates lifting for the King of glory), to Psalm 68:19 (God ascending on high), and to the Sinai theophany (Exodus 19:16-19) with its shofar blasts. The gathering of nations echoes Isaiah 2:2-3, where all nations stream to Zion. Paul's argument in Romans 4 that Abraham is the father of all who believe, not merely of circumcised Jews, follows the trajectory this psalm begins.

verses.text_kjv: To the chief Musician, A Psalm for the sons of Korah.
verses.rendering: For the choirmaster. A psalm of the Sons of Korah.
translator_notes: Verse 1 continues the psalm's thematic and structural development.

verses.text_hebrew: כׇּל־הָ֭עַמִּים תִּקְעוּ־כָ֑ף הָרִ֥יעוּ לֵ֝אלֹהִ֗ים בְּק֣וֹל רִנָּֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: O clap your hands, all ye people; shout unto God with the voice of triumph.
verses.rendering:
All you peoples, clap your hands!
Shout to God with a voice of ringing joy!
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm erupts with physical worship. kol ha-ammim tiq'u khaf ('all the peoples, strike the palm!') — the call goes out not to Israel alone but to all peoples on earth. The verb taqa ('to strike, to thrust, to clap') is the same word used for blasting a trumpet; here applied to the hand, it creates a thunderous communal ovation. hariu le-Elohim be-qol rinnah ('shout to God with a voice of joyful cry') — the verb rua ('to shout, to raise a war cry, to give a blast') is used for battle cries, coronation acclamations, and liturgical shouts. rinnah is the sound of piercing joy — the sharp, high-pitched cry that cuts through noise. This is not quiet reverence but full-volume, full-body exultation.
translator_notes: The universalism of kol ha-ammim ('all the peoples') is not hypothetical — it is an imperative. The psalm commands all nations to worship, not merely predicts that they will. This is a bold liturgical claim: the God of Israel deserves the worship of every human community.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־יְהוָ֣ה עֶלְי֣וֹן נוֹרָ֑א מֶ֥לֶךְ גָּ֝ד֗וֹל עַל־כׇּל־הָאָֽרֶץ׃
verses.text_kjv: For the LORD most high is terrible; he is a great King over all the earth.
verses.rendering:
For the LORD, the Most High, is awesome —
a great King over all the earth.
translator_notes: Three titles accumulate: YHVH ('the LORD' — the covenant name), Elyon ('Most High' — supreme over all), and nora ('awesome, fearsome, to be dreaded'). melekh gadol al kol ha-arets ('a great king over all the earth') is the enthronement declaration — God is not merely Israel's king but the king of the entire world. The title 'great king' (melekh gadol) echoes the Assyrian royal title sharru rabu, deliberately claiming for Israel's God the supremacy that earthly empires claimed for their rulers.

key_terms.semantic_range: fearsome, terrible, awe-inspiring, dreadful, to be revered
key_terms.note: nora (from yare, 'to fear') describes that which properly inspires fear and awe. When applied to God, it means God's presence is overwhelming — not frightening in a hostile sense but so far beyond human categories that the natural response is trembling reverence.

verses.text_hebrew: יַדְבֵּ֣ר עַמִּ֣ים תַּחְתֵּ֑ינוּ וּ֝לְאֻמִּ֗ים תַּ֣חַת רַגְלֵֽינוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: He shall subdue the people under us, and the nations under our feet.
verses.rendering:
He subdues peoples under us
and nations beneath our feet.
translator_notes: yadber ammim tachtenu ('he subdues peoples under us') uses davar in its rare sense of 'to drive, to subdue, to lead' (cf. Deuteronomy 33:3). The image is of conquest — but conquest achieved by God, not by Israel's military. The nations under Israel's feet reflects the ancient Near Eastern image of a conqueror placing his foot on the neck of the defeated (cf. Joshua 10:24).

verses.text_hebrew: יִבְחַר־לָ֥נוּ אֶת־נַחֲלָתֵ֑נוּ אֶ֥ת גְּא֨וֹן יַעֲקֹ֖ב אֲשֶׁר־אָהֵ֣ב סֶֽלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: He shall choose our inheritance for us, the excellency of Jacob whom he loved. Selah.
verses.rendering:
He chooses our inheritance for us —
the pride of Jacob whom he loves. Selah.
translator_notes: nachalatenu ('our inheritance') refers to the promised land, given as a permanent possession. ge'on Ya'aqov ('the pride/excellency of Jacob') likely refers to the land of Israel or to the Temple — the thing that makes Jacob's descendants distinct and honored. asher ahev ('whom he loved') — God's choice of this inheritance is rooted in love, not obligation.

verses.text_hebrew: עָלָ֣ה אֱ֭לֹהִים בִּתְרוּעָ֑ה יְ֝הוָ֗ה בְּק֣וֹל שׁוֹפָֽר׃
verses.text_kjv: God is gone up with a shout, the LORD with the sound of a trumpet.
verses.rendering:
God has gone up with a shout,
the LORD with the blast of a ram's horn!
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm's climactic moment: alah Elohim bitru'ah ('God has ascended with a shout'). The verb alah ('to go up') describes God ascending to his throne — either a heavenly enthronement or a liturgical reenactment in which the Ark was carried up to the Temple mount. The teru'ah ('shout, blast, acclamation') is the crowd's response to the king's coronation. YHVH be-qol shofar ('the LORD with the sound of the shofar') — the ram's horn blast that marked coronations (1 Kings 1:39, 2 Kings 9:13), the new year, and the divine theophany at Sinai (Exodus 19:16, 19). The double naming — Elohim and YHVH — identifies the ascending God with both the universal deity and the covenant LORD of Israel.
translator_notes: In Jewish liturgy, this verse grounds the practice of blowing the shofar on Rosh Hashanah — the day traditionally understood as the anniversary of God's enthronement as King. The psalm is recited seven times before the shofar blasts begin.

Christian liturgy applied this verse to the Ascension of Christ (Acts 1:9-11), reading the ascending God as the risen Lord returning to his heavenly throne. The early church fathers (particularly Augustine and Chrysostom) commented extensively on this connection.

key_terms.note: The shofar is the most ancient Israelite instrument, made from a ram's horn. Its blast signals God's presence (Sinai), the start of war (Joshua 6), the coronation of kings (1 Kings 1:34), and the beginning of sacred time (Leviticus 25:9, Rosh Hashanah). It is the sound that bridges heaven and earth.

verses.text_hebrew: זַמְּר֣וּ אֱלֹהִ֣ים זַמֵּ֑רוּ זַמְּר֖וּ לְמַלְכֵּ֣נוּ זַמֵּֽרוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Sing praises to God, sing praises: sing praises unto our King, sing praises.
verses.rendering:
Sing praise to God — sing praise!
Sing praise to our King — sing praise!
translator_notes: The fourfold repetition of zammeru ('sing praise!') is the psalm's ecstatic center. The verb zamar ('to sing, to make music') appears four times in rapid succession, creating a cascade of praise. The shift from Elohim ('God') to malkenu ('our King') in the parallel lines identifies God as Israel's personal sovereign — not merely the cosmic king but our king.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י מֶ֖לֶךְ כׇּל־הָאָ֥רֶץ אֱלֹהִ֗ים זַמְּר֥וּ מַשְׂכִּֽיל׃
verses.text_kjv: For God is the King of all the earth: sing ye praises with understanding.
verses.rendering:
For God is King of all the earth;
sing praise with a maskil!
translator_notes: melekh kol ha-arets ('king of all the earth') restates the universal claim of verse 3. zammeru maskil ('sing a maskil') — the maskil here may mean 'sing with understanding/skill' or 'sing an instructive song.' The five-fold occurrence of zamar in verses 7-8 (four imperatives plus this one) is unprecedented in the Psalter.

verses.text_hebrew: מָלַ֣ךְ אֱ֭לֹהִים עַל־גּוֹיִ֑ם אֱ֝לֹהִ֗ים יָשַׁ֤ב ׀ עַל־כִּסֵּ֬א קׇדְשֽׁוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: God reigneth over the heathen: God sitteth upon the throne of his holiness.
verses.rendering:
God reigns over the nations;
God sits on his holy throne.
verses.expanded_rendering: malakh Elohim al goyim ('God has become king over the nations') — the verb malakh can mean 'he reigns' (stative) or 'he has become king' (ingressive). In the enthronement psalms, the ingressive reading captures the coronation moment: God has just taken his throne. His rule is not over Israel alone but al goyim ('over the nations'). Elohim yashav al kisse qodsho ('God sits upon his holy throne') — yashav ('to sit, to dwell, to be enthroned') is the posture of authority. The throne is qodsho ('his holiness') — the throne itself is holy, set apart, radiating the sacred presence of the one who sits upon it.
translator_notes: The declaration malakh Elohim ('God reigns/has become king') echoes the enthronement shout YHVH malakh ('the LORD reigns') in Psalms 93:1, 96:10, 97:1, and 99:1. This formula is the theological heart of the enthronement psalm genre.

verses.text_hebrew: נְדִ֘יבֵ֤י עַמִּ֨ים ׀ נֶאֱסָ֗פוּ עַם֮ אֱלֹהֵ֢י אַבְ֫רָהָ֥ם כִּ֣י לֵ֭אלֹהִים מָֽגִנֵּי־אֶ֗רֶץ מְאֹ֣ד נַעֲלָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: The princes of the people are gathered together, even the people of the God of Abraham: for the shields of the earth belong unto God: he is greatly exalted.
verses.rendering:
The nobles of the peoples have gathered
as the people of the God of Abraham,
for the shields of the earth belong to God —
he is greatly exalted.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm's final verse is its most theologically expansive. nedive ammim ne'esafu ('the nobles of the peoples have gathered') — not Israelite nobles but the rulers of all nations. They have assembled as am Elohe Avraham ('the people of the God of Abraham') — they have become part of Abraham's family. This is the fulfillment of the Abrahamic promise: all the families of the earth blessed through Abraham (Genesis 12:3), the nations streaming to worship Abraham's God. The phrase maginne erets ('the shields of the earth') may refer to the rulers/protectors of the nations or to their military defenses; either way, they belong to God (le-Elohim). me'od na'alah ('he is greatly exalted') closes the psalm with the highest possible ascription: God is lifted up beyond all measure.
translator_notes: The identification of the gathered nations as am Elohe Avraham ('the people of the God of Abraham') rather than 'the people of Israel' is theologically significant. Abraham is the father of many nations (Genesis 17:5), not only of Israel. This psalm envisions the nations entering Abraham's covenant family — a vision that Paul will develop in Romans 4 and Galatians 3.

maginne erets ('shields of the earth') is an unusual phrase. magen can mean 'shield' (a defensive weapon) or 'patron, protector' (a metaphorical usage). If it means 'patrons/rulers,' the verse says earth's rulers belong to God. If 'shields,' the verse says earth's defenses are God's property. Either reading reinforces God's sovereignty.
